entries.shortExplanation: N이에요 / 예요 means "to be / am / is / are" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: N이에요 / 예요 means "to be / am / is / are". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use this for identity, names, jobs, places, and simple “it is...” sentences. It does not describe actions.

patternInfo.formNote: Use 이에요 after a final consonant and 예요 after a vowel.

studyGuide.title: Noun sentences
howToBuild: Choose a noun.
Use 이에요 after a final consonant and 예요 after a vowel.
Use 입니다 when the situation is very formal.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use this for identity, names, jobs, places, and simple “it is...” sentences. It does not describe actions.
studyGuide.learnerPath: After this, study 있어요 and 없어요 so you can say what something is and whether it exists.

entries.nuance: Use 이에요 after a final consonant and 예요 after a vowel.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use N이에요 / 예요 to express “to be / am / is / are” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: After this, study 있어요 and 없어요 so you can say what something is and whether it exists.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 학생이에요.

beginnerPath.title: 1. Replace the study symbol
beginnerPath.body: Choose a noun. The letters V and N are textbook symbols. They are never spoken aloud. Replace them with a real verb or noun.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 학생이에요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Check the situation before speaking
beginnerPath.body: Use this for identity, names, jobs, places, and simple “it is...” sentences. It does not describe actions.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Connect it back to word cards
beginnerPath.body: Open the linked words and find the same pattern inside their form panels. This makes grammar feel reusable instead of abstract.

classroomScript: Learner underlines the stem or noun that changes.
Learner reads two complete examples without saying the study symbols V or N.
Teacher gives one new word. Learner substitutes it into the same pattern.
Learner explains when the pattern is natural in plain English.
selfCheck: Can you say the pattern with one verb or noun from the linked cards?
Can you explain when this pattern is natural?
Can you avoid the “Watch out” mistake?

entries.shortExplanation: N이/가 있어요 means "there is / have" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: N이/가 있어요 means "there is / have". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for having time, money, reservations, friends, or for asking whether something exists in a place.

patternInfo.formNote: Use for existence or possession. 이 follows consonants; 가 follows vowels.

studyGuide.title: Existence and possession
howToBuild: Put the thing before 이/가.
Use 이 after a consonant and 가 after a vowel.
Add 있어요 for “there is” or “I have.”
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for having time, money, reservations, friends, or for asking whether something exists in a place.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Pair this with 없어요. Together they cover many beginner travel and classroom situations.

entries.nuance: Use for existence or possession. 이 follows consonants; 가 follows vowels.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use N이/가 있어요 to express “there is / have” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: Pair this with 없어요. Together they cover many beginner travel and classroom situations.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 시간이 있어요.

beginnerPath.title: 1. Replace the study symbol
beginnerPath.body: Put the thing before 이/가. The letters V and N are textbook symbols. They are never spoken aloud. Replace them with a real verb or noun.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 시간이 있어요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Check the situation before speaking
beginnerPath.body: Use it for having time, money, reservations, friends, or for asking whether something exists in a place.

entries.english: there is not / do not have
entries.shortExplanation: N이/가 없어요 means "there is not / do not have" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: N이/가 없어요 means "there is not / do not have". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for “I do not have time,” “there is no bathroom,” “there is no problem,” and similar survival sentences.

patternInfo.meaning: there is not / do not have
patternInfo.formNote: Use for absence or not having something.

studyGuide.title: Absence and not having
howToBuild: Put the missing thing before 이/가.
Use 없어요 to say it does not exist or you do not have it.
Add a time or place if needed.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for “I do not have time,” “there is no bathroom,” “there is no problem,” and similar survival sentences.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Learn it together with 있어요 so yes/no existence questions feel natural.

usagePhrases.en: I do not have a reservation.

entries.nuance: Use for absence or not having something.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use N이/가 없어요 to express “there is not / do not have” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.

Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: Learn it together with 있어요 so yes/no existence questions feel natural.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 시간이 없어요.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 시간이 없어요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Check the situation before speaking
beginnerPath.body: Use it for “I do not have time,” “there is no bathroom,” “there is no problem,” and similar survival sentences.

entries.shortExplanation: N 주세요 means "please give me N" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: N 주세요 means "please give me N". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use this in restaurants, cafes, stores, counters, and anywhere you are asking for a concrete item.

patternInfo.formNote: Use for ordering or asking for an item. Add 좀 when you want the request to sound a little softer.

studyGuide.title: Item requests
howToBuild: Name the item you want.
Add 주세요.
Add quantity words like 하나 or 한 잔 before 주세요 when needed.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use this in restaurants, cafes, stores, counters, and anywhere you are asking for a concrete item.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is different from V아/어 주세요. N 주세요 asks for a thing; V아/어 주세요 asks someone to do an action.

entries.nuance: Use for ordering or asking for an item. Add 좀 when you want the request to sound a little softer.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use N 주세요 to express “please give me N” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: This is different from V아/어 주세요. N 주세요 asks for a thing; V아/어 주세요 asks someone to do an action.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 물 주세요.

entries.shortExplanation: V고 싶어요 means "want to do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V고 싶어요 means "want to do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for your own wants: 먹고 싶어요, 가고 싶어요, 쉬고 싶어요. It sounds softer and more useful than directly saying “I want X” in many beginner situations.

patternInfo.formNote: Attach 고 싶어요 to a verb stem to say what you want to do.

studyGuide.title: Desire: want to do
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the dictionary verb.
Add 고 싶어요.
Put the object before the verb if the action needs one.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for your own wants: 먹고 싶어요, 가고 싶어요, 쉬고 싶어요. It sounds softer and more useful than directly saying “I want X” in many beginner situations.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Practice this after you know the polite present form. It is one of the fastest ways to turn vocabulary into real conversation.

entries.nuance: Attach 고 싶어요 to a verb stem to say what you want to do.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V고 싶어요 to express “want to do” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.

lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 먹고 싶어요.

entries.shortExplanation: V고 싶지 않아요 means "do not want to do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V고 싶지 않아요 means "do not want to do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use this when you do not want to eat, go, wait, talk, or continue. It is often gentler than 싫어요.

patternInfo.formNote: Negative desire. Softer than saying 싫어요 in many situations.

studyGuide.title: Negative desire
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb.
Add 고 싶지 않아요.
Add 지금은 or 오늘은 to make the refusal softer.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use this when you do not want to eat, go, wait, talk, or continue. It is often gentler than 싫어요.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Use this with simple time words first, then compare it with 안 V해요, which only says you do not do something.

usagePhrases.en: I do not want to eat.
I do not want to go.
I do not want to speak.
I do not want to wait.

examples.en: I do not want to eat right now.
I do not want to go today.
I do not want to talk about that.

entries.nuance: Negative desire. Softer than saying 싫어요 in many situations.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V고 싶지 않아요 to express “do not want to do” in a short real-life sentence.

Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.

lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 먹고 싶지 않아요.

entries.shortExplanation: Vㄹ/을 수 있어요 means "can do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: Vㄹ/을 수 있어요 means "can do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for skills, schedule possibility, permission-like possibility, and practical questions such as 카드로 살 수 있어요?

patternInfo.formNote: Use for ability or possibility. The final sound decides ㄹ 수 있어요 after vowels and 을 수 있어요 after consonants.

studyGuide.title: Ability and possibility
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb.
Use ㄹ 수 있어요 after a vowel ending.
Use 을 수 있어요 after a consonant ending.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for skills, schedule possibility, permission-like possibility, and practical questions such as 카드로 살 수 있어요?
studyGuide.learnerPath: Study this with 못 V해요 so you can clearly say what is possible and what is not possible.

usagePhrases.en: I can buy it by card.

examples.en: Can I buy it by card?

entries.nuance: Use for ability or possibility. The final sound decides ㄹ 수 있어요 after vowels and 을 수 있어요 after consonants.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use Vㄹ/을 수 있어요 to express “can do” in a short real-life sentence.

entries.shortExplanation: 못 + verb means "cannot / be unable to" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: 못 + verb means "cannot / be unable to". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for inability, schedule conflicts, food restrictions, or language limits: 못 가요, 못 먹어요, 한국어를 잘 못해요. It says “I cannot” because of a real limit, not simply “I choose not to.”

patternInfo.formNote: For most verbs, put 못 before the normal verb form: 가요 becomes 못 가요. For 하다 verbs, use 못 해요 or noun 못 해요. Never make forms like 못 가해요.

studyGuide.title: Cannot / unable to
howToBuild: For most verbs, put 못 before the normal polite verb: 가요 becomes 못 가요, 먹어요 becomes 못 먹어요.
Do not add 해요 to a non-하다 verb. It is 못 가요, not 못 가해요.
For 하다 verbs, use 못 해요 or noun 못 해요: 해요 becomes 못 해요, 공부해요 becomes 공부 못 해요.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for inability, schedule conflicts, food restrictions, or language limits: 못 가요, 못 먹어요, 한국어를 잘 못해요. It says “I cannot” because of a real limit, not simply “I choose not to.”
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is a survival pattern. First memorize real examples like 못 가요, 못 먹어요, and 말 못 해요 before trying to build new ones.

entries.nuance: For most verbs, put 못 before the normal verb form: 가요 becomes 못 가요. For 하다 verbs, use 못 해요 or noun 못 해요. Never make forms like 못 가해요.
commonMistakes: Do not attach 해요 to every verb. Say 못 가요, not 못 가해요.
Memorize two complete examples before trying to create new sentences from this pattern.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use 못 + verb to express “cannot / be unable to” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: This is a survival pattern. First memorize real examples like 못 가요, 못 먹어요, and 말 못 해요 before trying to build new ones.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 못 가요.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Connect it back to word cards

entries.english: do not do / am not doing
entries.shortExplanation: 안 V해요 means "do not do / am not doing" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: 안 V해요 means "do not do / am not doing". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for ordinary negatives: 안 가요, 안 먹어요, 공부 안 해요. It is short, common, and conversational.

patternInfo.meaning: do not do / am not doing
patternInfo.formNote: Short negative. With noun + 하다 verbs, the 안 often goes between the noun and 하다.

studyGuide.title: Short negative
howToBuild: Put 안 before most simple verbs.
For noun + 하다 verbs, put 안 between the noun and 하다.
Use it with a time word to make the meaning clear.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for ordinary negatives: 안 가요, 안 먹어요, 공부 안 해요. It is short, common, and conversational.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Compare it with 못 V해요. 안 often means you do not do it; 못 means you cannot.

examples.en: I do not work on weekends.

entries.nuance: Short negative. With noun + 하다 verbs, the 안 often goes between the noun and 하다.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use 안 V해요 to express “do not do / am not doing” in a short real-life sentence.

beginnerPath.body: Put 안 before most simple verbs. The letters V and N are textbook symbols. They are never spoken aloud. Replace them with a real verb or noun.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 안 가요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Check the situation before speaking
beginnerPath.body: Use it for ordinary negatives: 안 가요, 안 먹어요, 공부 안 해요. It is short, common, and conversational.

entries.shortExplanation: V지 않아요 means "do not / is not" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V지 않아요 means "do not / is not". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it with both verbs and adjectives: 가지 않아요, 맵지 않아요, 어렵지 않아요. It sounds more careful than the short 안 negative, so it is useful in explanations, writing, or polite clarification.

patternInfo.formNote: Longer negative. It sounds a little more careful or written than 안.

studyGuide.title: Careful negative
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb or adjective.
Add 지 않아요.
Use it when you want a more careful or written-sounding negative.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it with both verbs and adjectives: 가지 않아요, 맵지 않아요, 어렵지 않아요. It sounds more careful than the short 안 negative, so it is useful in explanations, writing, or polite clarification.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Beginners can use 안 first, then learn 지 않아요 for careful speech and longer sentences.

examples.en: Korean is not easy, but it is fun.

entries.nuance: Longer negative. It sounds a little more careful or written than 안.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V지 않아요 to express “do not / is not” in a short real-life sentence.

lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 가지 않아요.

entries.shortExplanation: V아/어야 해요 means "have to / must" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V아/어야 해요 means "have to / must". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for rules, appointments, health needs, work, school, and personal plans: 가야 해요, 먹어야 해요, 예약해야 해요.

patternInfo.formNote: Use for obligation or necessary action. It can describe rules, plans, health needs, or things you personally must do.

studyGuide.title: Obligation: have to
howToBuild: Choose the 아/어 form of the verb.
Add 야 해요.
Use a reason before it when you want to explain why.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for rules, appointments, health needs, work, school, and personal plans: 가야 해요, 먹어야 해요, 예약해야 해요.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is a core survival pattern because it lets you explain constraints instead of only saying yes or no.

examples.en: I have to go early tomorrow.
I have a test, so I have to study.

entries.nuance: Use for obligation or necessary action. It can describe rules, plans, health needs, or things you personally must do.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V아/어야 해요 to express “have to / must” in a short real-life sentence.

Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.

lesson.teacherNote: This is a core survival pattern because it lets you explain constraints instead of only saying yes or no.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 가야 해요.

entries.shortExplanation: V지 않아도 돼요 means "do not have to do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V지 않아도 돼요 means "do not have to do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it to reassure someone or explain that something is optional: 기다리지 않아도 돼요, 예약하지 않아도 돼요.

patternInfo.formNote: Use to say something is not necessary. It sounds like permission or relief, not like refusing to do something.

studyGuide.title: Not necessary / do not have to
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb.
Add 지 않아도 돼요.
Use it to remove an obligation.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it to reassure someone or explain that something is optional: 기다리지 않아도 돼요, 예약하지 않아도 돼요.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Study it right after 아/어야 해요. The pair gives you “must” and “do not have to.”

usagePhrases.en: You do not have to go.
You do not have to wait.
You do not need to reserve.
You do not have to study.

examples.en: You do not have to go today.
You do not have to wait here.
You do not need to make a reservation.

entries.nuance: Use to say something is not necessary. It sounds like permission or relief, not like refusing to do something.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V지 않아도 돼요 to express “do not have to do” in a short real-life sentence.

Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.

lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 가지 않아도 돼요.

entries.english: may I / is it okay to do?
entries.shortExplanation: V아/어도 돼요? means "may I / is it okay to do?" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V아/어도 돼요? means "may I / is it okay to do?". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it before sitting, entering, taking photos, using a card, or doing anything where permission matters.

patternInfo.meaning: may I / is it okay to do?
patternInfo.formNote: Ask permission politely. Use a place or object before it when the situation needs to be clear.

studyGuide.title: Permission question
howToBuild: Use the 아/어 form of the verb.
Add 도 돼요?
Add 여기서, 지금, or the object if the question needs context.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it before sitting, entering, taking photos, using a card, or doing anything where permission matters.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is one of the most useful travel patterns. Pair it with 지 마세요 so you understand both permission and prohibition.

entries.nuance: Ask permission politely. Use a place or object before it when the situation needs to be clear.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

entries.shortExplanation: V지 마세요 means "please do not do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V지 마세요 means "please do not do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for rules, warnings, and gentle requests: 사진 찍지 마세요, 가지 마세요, 걱정하지 마세요. You will see it on signs and hear it from staff, but use it carefully because it can sound like an instruction.

patternInfo.formNote: Polite prohibition or warning. It can appear on signs, from staff, or as a gentle request between people.

studyGuide.title: Prohibition: please do not
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb.
Add 지 마세요.
Use a place or time phrase if the rule needs context.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for rules, warnings, and gentle requests: 사진 찍지 마세요, 가지 마세요, 걱정하지 마세요. You will see it on signs and hear it from staff, but use it carefully because it can sound like an instruction.
studyGuide.learnerPath: You need this mostly for understanding signs and staff instructions. Use it carefully when speaking to others.

usagePhrases.en: Please do not open the door.

examples.en: Please do not take photos here.

entries.nuance: Polite prohibition or warning. It can appear on signs, from staff, or as a gentle request between people.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V지 마세요 to express “please do not do” in a short real-life sentence.

entries.shortExplanation: V아/어 주세요 means "please do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V아/어 주세요 means "please do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it when asking someone to do an action: 도와주세요, 열어 주세요, 다시 말해 주세요, 사진 찍어 주세요.

patternInfo.formNote: Polite request for someone to do an action.

studyGuide.title: Action requests
howToBuild: Use the 아/어 form of the verb.
Add 주세요.
Add 좀 to soften everyday requests.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it when asking someone to do an action: 도와주세요, 열어 주세요, 다시 말해 주세요, 사진 찍어 주세요.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is not the same as N 주세요. Learn both so you can ask for items and actions naturally.

entries.nuance: Polite request for someone to do an action.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V아/어 주세요 to express “please do” in a short real-life sentence.

entries.shortExplanation: Vㄹ/을까요? means "shall we / should I?" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: Vㄹ/을까요? means "shall we / should I?". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for suggestions and gentle decision questions: 갈까요?, 먹을까요?, 기다릴까요? It can mean “shall we” when deciding together, or “should I” when asking what action you should take.

patternInfo.formNote: Use for suggestions or asking what to do.

studyGuide.title: Suggestion / checking what to do
howToBuild: Use ㄹ까요 after a vowel-ending stem.
Use 을까요 after a consonant-ending stem.
Add 같이 or 지금 when useful.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for suggestions and gentle decision questions: 갈까요?, 먹을까요?, 기다릴까요? It can mean “shall we” when deciding together, or “should I” when asking what action you should take.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This turns verbs into conversation starters. It is especially useful after you know 가다, 먹다, 기다리다, and 주문하다.

entries.nuance: Use for suggestions or asking what to do.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 갈까요?. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 3. Check the situation before speaking
beginnerPath.body: Use it for suggestions and gentle decision questions: 갈까요?, 먹을까요?, 기다릴까요? It can mean “shall we” when deciding together, or “should I” when asking what action you should take.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Connect it back to word cards
beginnerPath.body: Open the linked words and find the same pattern inside their form panels. This makes grammar feel reusable instead of abstract.

classroomScript: Learner underlines the stem or noun that changes.
Learner reads two complete examples without saying the study symbols V or N.
Teacher gives one new word. Learner substitutes it into the same pattern.
Learner explains when the pattern is natural in plain English.
selfCheck: Can you say the pattern with one verb or noun from the linked cards?
Can you explain when this pattern is natural?
Can you avoid the “Watch out” mistake?

entries.shortExplanation: Vㄹ/을게요 means "I will do" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: Vㄹ/을게요 means "I will do". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it when you decide or promise your own action: 제가 할게요, 전화할게요, 기다릴게요. It is common when responding to someone and volunteering what you will do next.

patternInfo.formNote: Speaker’s promise or decision. Use it when you choose what you will do in response to the situation.

studyGuide.title: Speaker decision / promise
howToBuild: Use ㄹ게요 after a vowel-ending stem.
Use 을게요 after a consonant-ending stem.
Use it only for what the speaker will do.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it when you decide or promise your own action: 제가 할게요, 전화할게요, 기다릴게요. It is common when responding to someone and volunteering what you will do next.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Do not use this to predict another person. It is about the speaker’s choice.

entries.nuance: Speaker’s promise or decision. Use it when you choose what you will do in response to the situation.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use Vㄹ/을게요 to express “I will do” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: Do not use this to predict another person. It is about the speaker’s choice.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 갈게요.

beginnerPath.body: Use ㄹ게요 after a vowel-ending stem. The letters V and N are textbook symbols. They are never spoken aloud. Replace them with a real verb or noun.

beginnerPath.title: 2. Memorize two complete examples
beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 갈게요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

beginnerPath.title: 4. Connect it back to word cards

entries.shortExplanation: V아서/어서 means "because / so" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V아서/어서 means "because / so". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it to explain why something happened: 바빠서 못 가요, 아파서 쉬어요, 늦어서 죄송합니다. It lets beginners connect a reason to a result without making two separate sentences.

patternInfo.formNote: Connect a reason to a result. Do not use it for commands or suggestions in formal grammar.

studyGuide.title: Reason connector
howToBuild: Use 아서/어서 after the first verb or adjective.
Put the result after it.
Use 그래서 in your English brain: reason, so result.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it to explain why something happened: 바빠서 못 가요, 아파서 쉬어요, 늦어서 죄송합니다. It lets beginners connect a reason to a result without making two separate sentences.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This makes your Korean sound connected instead of like separate flashcard sentences.

usagePhrases.en: I am busy, so I cannot go.
I am sick, so I rest.
It is expensive, so I am not buying it.

examples.en: I am busy, so I cannot go.
I am sick, so I go to the hospital.

entries.nuance: Connect a reason to a result. Do not use it for commands or suggestions in formal grammar.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V아서/어서 to express “because / so” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: This makes your Korean sound connected instead of like separate flashcard sentences.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 바빠서 못 가요.

entries.shortExplanation: V고 means "and / and then" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V고 means "and / and then". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it to list actions or describe sequence: 먹고 가요, 씻고 자요, 싸고 좋아요. It is one of the easiest ways to make longer sentences from vocabulary you already know.

patternInfo.formNote: Connect actions or descriptions. It can simply list two ideas, or show one action followed by another.

studyGuide.title: And / sequence connector
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the first verb or adjective.
Add 고.
Put the next action or description after it.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it to list actions or describe sequence: 먹고 가요, 씻고 자요, 싸고 좋아요. It is one of the easiest ways to make longer sentences from vocabulary you already know.
studyGuide.learnerPath: Use this after you can make short sentences. It helps combine two simple ideas into one sentence.

examples.en: I eat and go to school.
This bag is cheap and good.

entries.nuance: Connect actions or descriptions. It can simply list two ideas, or show one action followed by another.
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V고 to express “and / and then” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: Use this after you can make short sentences. It helps combine two simple ideas into one sentence.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 먹고 가요.

beginnerPath.body: Start with complete phrases such as 먹고 가요. After two examples feel natural, substitute only one word at a time.

entries.shortExplanation: V면 means "if / when" as a reusable Korean sentence pattern.
entries.explanation: V면 means "if / when". This is a reusable sentence pattern, not a vocabulary word. Replace the marked part with a real noun, verb stem, or adjective stem, then choose the speech level for the person you are talking to. For v1 study, focus on the polite form first and memorize complete examples before creating your own sentences.
entries.learnerPriority: First memorize two complete polite examples. Then substitute one word at a time and check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
entries.contextHint: Use it for conditions: 시간이 있으면 전화해 주세요, 비싸면 안 사요, 아프면 쉬어야 해요. It helps you say what should happen depending on time, price, health, or permission.

patternInfo.formNote: Set a condition. Beginners can read it as “if,” while context sometimes makes it closer to “when.”

studyGuide.title: Condition: if / when
howToBuild: Remove 다 from the verb or adjective.
Add 면.
Put the result, request, or advice after it.
studyGuide.whenToUse: Use it for conditions: 시간이 있으면 전화해 주세요, 비싸면 안 사요, 아프면 쉬어야 해요. It helps you say what should happen depending on time, price, health, or permission.
studyGuide.learnerPath: This is a bridge into longer Korean. Keep the first half short while you are a beginner.

usagePhrases.en: If it is expensive, I do not buy it.
If it is okay, let us go.

examples.en: If you have time, please call.
If it is expensive, I do not buy it.
If you are sick, you should rest.

entries.nuance: Set a condition. Beginners can read it as “if,” while context sometimes makes it closer to “when.”
commonMistakes: Do not say the study symbol V or N out loud; replace it with a real verb stem or noun.
Check whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective before using it.

lesson.canDo: Use V면 to express “if / when” in a short real-life sentence.
studyFlow: Read the pattern explanation before memorizing the shape.
Study the build steps and identify whether the pattern attaches to a noun, verb, or adjective.
Practice the usage phrases as substitutions, changing only one part at a time.
Open one linked word and see the same pattern inside a verb card.
lesson.teacherNote: This is a bridge into longer Korean. Keep the first half short while you are a beginner.
lesson.pronunciationTip: Pattern labels are study tools. In speech, practice the full example phrase such as 시간이 있으면.

beginnerPath.body: Open the linked words and find the same pattern inside their form panels. This makes grammar feel reusable instead of abstract.
